title: Chapter 5 (expert) — Chaining ambiences & the per-biome soundtrack
hero.title: The right moment, not just the right sound
hero.lead: The finishing touch of procedural sound: chaining two ambiences without cutting a gust by waiting for a lull, then the per-biome soundtrack and its round transition — crossfading the volumes and gliding the tempo along the same curve.
s1.p1: In random play, each character plays for a while, then another takes over. Since wind is a continuous loop, we avoid cutting it mid-swell: once the duration elapses, we wait for a lull (a dip in level) before switching, with a crossfade of about 1.4 s. A 2.5 s cap guarantees it always eventually changes.
s1.code.cap: the character switch, timed to a lull
s1.p2: The result: you never hear a gust cut off abruptly — the transition slips into the wind's relative quiet.
s1.callout.tip: The moral. Good procedural sound isn't only synthesis: it's also when you trigger things. Waiting for the right moment often beats a longer fade.
s3.h2: And that wraps up the notebook!
s3.lead: From the game loop to synthesized sound, you've made the full tour of Tower Duel.
s3.p1: Five batches, three reading levels, a handful of simulators: you now know how a small web game comes to life, screen after screen, gust after gust. Thanks for reading all the way through.
s2.try: Turn the sound on: everything is synthesized live, no audio files.
s2.p1: Each biome has its own sonic climate, offered in several proposals. During a match, a biome's two ambiences alternate from round to round; the final round has its own music. When the scene slides to the next hill, the music follows: a crossfade of the volumes and a tempo glide (BPM) along the same smoothstep curve, so both tracks stay in sync through the transition.
s2.p2: The catalog keeps every proposal — including the five Desert ones. The mixer plays the playlist chosen per biome (Desert: Mirage and Fournaise in rotation, Simoun for the finale). Pick a biome, then run through the rounds.
